summary:
The Siege of Sevastopol (October 1854 – September 1855) was the central and most costly episode of the [[event:2547|Crimean War]], a nearly year-long investment of the main Russian naval base on the Black Sea by British, French, Ottoman, and Sardinian forces. The siege became emblematic of the industrial-age transformation of warfare — with railway logistics, telegraph communications, rifled firearms, and explosive shells all playing decisive roles — and of the catastrophic mismanagement and suffering that accompanied it.

British and French forces landed in Crimea in September 1854 and defeated a Russian army at the Alma River, then settled into siege operations against Sevastopol's formidable earthwork defenses, which were continuously reinforced and expanded under the brilliant engineering direction of Eduard Todleben. The siege settled into a grinding attritional campaign through the winter of 1854–55 — a winter that killed far more men through disease, cold, and supply failures than the fighting did. The scandal of British soldiers dying of preventable illness and supply failures was exposed by war correspondent William Howard Russell, and Florence Nightingale's nursing mission to Scutari responded to the public outcry.

Russian sorties and allied counter-operations — including the [[event:3761|Battle of Balaclava]] and the Battle of Inkerman — failed to break the siege, while allied assaults on the city's bastions were repeatedly repulsed with heavy losses. Sevastopol finally fell in September 1855 when French troops stormed the Malakhov bastion, the key to the city's defenses. The Russians evacuated and scuttled their remaining fleet. The fall of Sevastopol effectively ended the Crimean War, leading to the [[event:3762|Treaty of Paris of 1856]].
